questions.question:
Which one or more of the following CPU scheduling algorithms can potentially cause starvation?
A. First-in First-Out
B. Round Robin
C. Priority Scheduling
D. Shortest Job First
questions.to_find: Which scheduling algorithms can potentially cause starvation

understand.plain: Starvation occurs when a process waits indefinitely because other processes keep getting the CPU. Some scheduling policies are fair (every process eventually runs), while others can keep low-priority or long processes waiting forever if the right mix of other processes keeps arriving.

keywords.explain: A situation where a process never gets CPU time because higher-priority or shorter processes continuously preempt or bypass it.
keywords.example: A low-priority process in Priority Scheduling may never run if high-priority processes keep arriving.

keywords.explain: Schedules the process with the smallest burst time next; long jobs can starve if short jobs continuously arrive.
keywords.example: A 100-ms job never runs if 1-ms jobs keep arriving.

keywords.explain: Assigns a priority to each process and always runs the highest-priority ready process; low-priority processes can starve.
keywords.example: Process with priority 10 never runs while processes with priority 1 keep arriving.

understand.visual_alt: Four scheduling algorithms compared on the starvation property: FIFO and Round Robin are starvation-free; Priority Scheduling and SJF can cause starvation.

given.aim: Identify which CPU scheduling algorithms can cause starvation.

terms.meaning: Processes are served in arrival order; every process eventually reaches the front.
terms.example: Three processes arrive; all three run in order — no starvation.
terms.connects: Because order is fixed, no process can be bypassed indefinitely.

terms.meaning: Each process gets a fixed time quantum in rotation; all ready processes share CPU fairly.
terms.example: With quantum q, every process runs within n*q time where n is the number of processes.
terms.connects: Cyclic scheduling prevents any process from waiting forever.

terms.meaning: Highest-priority process runs next; low-priority processes can be indefinitely delayed.
terms.example: A process with priority 10 starves if priority-1 processes keep arriving.
terms.connects: No lower bound on wait time for low-priority processes — starvation possible.

terms.meaning: Process with the shortest remaining (or expected) burst time runs next; very long jobs can starve.
terms.example: A 1000-ms job starves if 5-ms jobs keep arriving at any rate.
terms.connects: Long jobs have no guarantee of ever being scheduled — starvation possible.

given.plan: Analyse each algorithm: does it guarantee every process will eventually run? FIFO and Round Robin are order-based/time-shared and guarantee progress; Priority Scheduling and SJF both make scheduling decisions based on job characteristics that can permanently favour some processes over others.

steps.title: Check FIFO and Round Robin for starvation
steps.apply: FIFO serves processes strictly in arrival order — every process eventually becomes the head of the queue and runs. Round Robin cycles through all ready processes in fixed-size quanta — every process gets CPU time within a bounded interval. Neither algorithm can cause starvation.
steps.note: FIFO can cause convoy effect (long wait times) but NOT starvation.

steps.title: Check Priority Scheduling and SJF for starvation
steps.apply: Priority Scheduling always runs the highest-priority ready process. If high-priority processes keep arriving, a low-priority process may never run — classic starvation. SJF always picks the shortest burst next. If short jobs keep arriving, a long job may never be scheduled — starvation. Both C (Priority) and D (SJF) can cause starvation.
steps.note: Aging (gradually increasing priority of waiting processes) is the standard remedy for starvation in both algorithms.

solution.result: C. Priority Scheduling and D. Shortest Job First can potentially cause starvation.

questions.formula_note: Starvation property of scheduling algorithms: algorithms that permanently favour certain processes (by priority or burst length) can starve others; fair-share algorithms (FIFO, RR) cannot.

questions.question:
Which of the following process state transitions is/are NOT possible?
A. Running to Ready
B. Waiting to Running
C. Ready to Waiting
D. Running to Terminated
questions.to_find: Which process state transitions are NOT possible in the standard process state model

understand.plain: A process moves between states (New, Ready, Running, Waiting/Blocked, Terminated) according to specific rules. Some transitions are legal (e.g., a running process can be preempted to Ready), but others violate the model (e.g., a Waiting process cannot jump directly to Running — it must go through Ready first).

keywords.explain: The standard five-state model showing valid transitions: New->Ready->Running->Terminated; Running->Ready (preemption); Running->Waiting (I/O); Waiting->Ready (I/O complete).
keywords.example: A process doing disk I/O moves Running -> Waiting, then after I/O completes moves Waiting -> Ready.

keywords.explain: A process is waiting for an event (I/O, signal). It cannot use the CPU and must move to Ready before Running.
keywords.example: A process blocked on keyboard input is in Waiting state.

understand.visual_alt: Standard process state diagram showing valid transitions: Ready->Running, Running->Ready (preemption), Running->Waiting, Waiting->Ready (I/O done), Running->Terminated. The invalid transitions B (Waiting->Running) and C (Ready->Waiting) are not shown.

given.aim: Identify which transitions in the process state diagram are NOT allowed.

terms.meaning: Preemption: the scheduler removes the CPU from a running process and moves it to the ready queue.
terms.example: Timer interrupt fires; process moved from Running to Ready.
terms.connects: Valid transition — used in preemptive scheduling.

terms.meaning: A blocked process cannot go directly to Running; it must first move to Ready, then be selected by the scheduler.
terms.example: After I/O completes, process goes Waiting->Ready, not Waiting->Running.
terms.connects: Invalid transition — violates the state model.

terms.meaning: A process in Ready state has not yet started executing, so it cannot issue an I/O request or block itself.
terms.example: A process can only call a blocking syscall while Running, not while Ready.
terms.connects: Invalid transition — only a Running process can enter the Waiting state.

terms.meaning: A running process completes or is killed, moving directly to Terminated.
terms.example: Process exits via _exit() syscall — transitions Running->Terminated.

given.plan: Map each option against the five-state process model. Only transitions that appear as edges in the standard diagram are valid. Waiting->Running and Ready->Waiting are not edges in the model.

steps.title: Verify valid transitions (A and D)
steps.apply: A. Running->Ready: Valid — this is preemption (timer interrupt or higher-priority process arrives). D. Running->Terminated: Valid — process completes execution or is killed by OS/user.
steps.note: Both A and D are standard edges in the five-state process model.

steps.title: Identify invalid transitions (B and C)
steps.apply: B. Waiting->Running: NOT possible. When an I/O event completes, the OS moves the process from Waiting to READY, adds it to the ready queue, and the scheduler later picks it to run. A process cannot jump from Waiting directly to Running. C. Ready->Waiting: NOT possible. A process in the Ready state is waiting for the CPU only — it has not started executing. Blocking operations (I/O calls) can only be issued by a Running process. Therefore a Ready process cannot transition to Waiting.
steps.note: These two transitions are absent from all standard OS process-state diagrams.

solution.result: B (Waiting to Running) and C (Ready to Waiting) are NOT possible transitions.

questions.formula_note: Five-state process model (New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated): only Running processes can issue blocking operations (->Waiting) and only the scheduler can promote a Ready/Waiting process to Running (via Ready queue).

questions.question:
Consider a single processor system with four processes A, B, C, D, represented as given below, where for each process the first value is its arrival time, and the second value is its burst time.
A(0, 10), B(2, 6), C(4, 3), D(6, 7)
Which one of the following options gives the average waiting times when preemptive Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) CPU and Non-Preemptive Shortest Job First (NP-SJF) CPU scheduling algorithms are used?
A. SRTF = 6, NP-SJF = 7
B. SRTF = 6, NP-SJF = 7.5
C. SRTF = 7, NP-SJF = 7.5
D. SRTF = 7, NP-SJF = 8.5
questions.to_find: Average waiting times under SRTF and Non-Preemptive SJF for the given four processes

understand.plain: We have four processes arriving at different times with given burst times. We simulate two scheduling algorithms: SRTF (always run whichever ready process has the least remaining burst, preempting if needed) and Non-Preemptive SJF (at each scheduling point, pick the ready process with the shortest burst and run it to completion). Then compute average waiting time for each.

keywords.explain: Preemptive version of SJF: at every instant, the process with the least remaining burst time runs. A new arrival can preempt the current process if it has a shorter remaining time.
keywords.example: Process running with 8 ms left is preempted by a new process with only 3 ms burst.

keywords.explain: At each scheduling decision (when CPU becomes free), pick the ready process with the smallest total burst time and run it to completion without interruption.
keywords.example: CPU free at t=10; three processes waiting with bursts 6, 3, 7 — pick the one with burst 3.

keywords.explain: Total time a process spends in the ready queue waiting for the CPU = Finish Time - Arrival Time - Burst Time.
keywords.example: Process arrives at 2, finishes at 11, burst = 6 => waiting = 11 - 2 - 6 = 3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 560 160' width='560' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='20' class='lbl ink'>SRTF Gantt:</text><rect x='10' y='30' width='20' height='25' class='hi'/><text x='20' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='sub style='fill:#1D9E75'/><text x='65' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white'>C</text><rect x='80' y='30' width='40' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>B</text><rect x='120' y='30' width='70' height='25' class='no' style='fill:#D85A30'/><text x='155' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white'>D</text><rect x='190' y='30' width='80' height='25' class='hi'/><text x='230' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>A</text><text x='10' y='75' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='28' y='75' class='sub mut'>2</text><text x='48' y='75' class='sub mut'>4</text><text x='75' y='75' class='sub mut'>7</text><text x='115' y='75' class='sub mut'>11</text><text x='185' y='75' class='sub mut'>18</text><text x='265' y='75' class='sub mut'>26</text><text x='10' y='100' class='lbl ink'>NP-SJF Gantt:</text><rect x='10' y='110' width='100' height='25' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='127' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>A</text><rect x='110' y='110' width='30' height='25' class='ok' style='fill:#1D9E75'/><text x='125' y='127' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white'>C</text><rect x='140' y='110' width='60' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='170' y='127' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>B</text><rect x='200' y='110' width='70' height='25' class='no' style='fill:#D85A30'/><text x='235' y='127' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white'>D</text><text x='10' y='152' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='107' y='152' class='sub mut'>10</text><text x='137' y='152' class='sub mut'>13</text><text x='195' y='152' class='sub mut'>19</text><text x='267' y='152' class='sub mut'>26</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Gantt charts for SRTF (A runs 0-2, B runs 2-4, C runs 4-7, B runs 7-11, D runs 11-18, A runs 18-26) and NP-SJF (A runs 0-10, C runs 10-13, B runs 13-19, D runs 19-26).

given.aim: Simulate SRTF and NP-SJF for the four processes and compute average waiting time for each.

terms.meaning: A arrives at t=0 with burst 10; B arrives at t=2 with burst 6; C arrives at t=4 with burst 3; D arrives at t=6 with burst 7.
terms.example: A(0,10) means A arrives at time 0 and needs 10 ms of CPU.
terms.connects: These drive all scheduling decisions in both algorithms.

terms.meaning: Waiting Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time - Burst Time
terms.example: If B finishes at 11, arrives at 2, burst 6: WT = 11-2-6 = 3.
terms.connects: Used to compute individual and average waiting times.

terms.meaning: Whenever a new process arrives, compare its burst with the remaining time of the current process; if new < remaining, preempt.
terms.example: At t=2, B arrives (burst=6) vs A's remaining=8; 6<8 so preempt A.
terms.connects: Determines the SRTF Gantt chart structure.

given.plan: For SRTF: simulate time step by step, preempting whenever a shorter process arrives. For NP-SJF: at each CPU-free moment, select the shortest burst among all available processes and run to completion. Compute waiting times from the Gantt chart using WT = CT - AT - BT.

steps.title: SRTF Simulation
steps.apply:
t=0: Only A ready (burst=10). A starts.
t=2: B arrives (burst=6). A remaining=8. 6<8 => preempt A. B runs.
t=4: C arrives (burst=3). B remaining=4. 3<4 => preempt B. C runs.
t=6: D arrives (burst=7). C remaining=1. 1<7 => no preemption. C continues.
t=7: C finishes. Ready: A(rem=8), B(rem=4), D(burst=7). Shortest=B(4). B runs.
t=11: B finishes. Ready: A(rem=8), D(burst=7). Shortest=D(7). D runs.
t=18: D finishes. Only A(rem=8) left. A runs.
t=26: A finishes.

Completion times: A=26, B=11, C=7, D=18.
WT(A) = 26-0-10 = 16
WT(B) = 11-2-6 = 3
WT(C) = 7-4-3 = 0
WT(D) = 18-6-7 = 5
Average WT(SRTF) = (16+3+0+5)/4 = 24/4 = 6
steps.note: C has zero waiting time as it runs immediately upon arrival with the smallest remaining burst.

steps.title: Non-Preemptive SJF Simulation
steps.apply:
t=0: Only A ready. A runs (non-preemptive). A runs 0 to 10.
t=10: A finishes. Ready: B(burst=6), C(burst=3), D(burst=7). Shortest=C(3). C runs 10-13.
t=13: C finishes. Ready: B(burst=6), D(burst=7). Shortest=B(6). B runs 13-19.
t=19: B finishes. Only D left. D runs 19-26.

Completion times: A=10, B=19, C=13, D=26.
WT(A) = 10-0-10 = 0
WT(B) = 19-2-6 = 11
WT(C) = 13-4-3 = 6
WT(D) = 26-6-7 = 13
Average WT(NP-SJF) = (0+11+6+13)/4 = 30/4 = 7.5
steps.note: A has no waiting time because it is the only process at t=0. D has the longest wait because it has the largest burst among the last three processes.

solution.result: SRTF average waiting time = 6, NP-SJF average waiting time = 7.5. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Waiting Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time - Burst Time; SRTF always selects the process with the minimum remaining burst (preemptive); NP-SJF selects the minimum burst at each scheduling event and runs to completion.

questions.question:
A computer has two processors, M1 and M2. Four processes with CPU bursts of 20, 16, 25, and 10 milliseconds, respectively, arrive at the same time and these are the only processes in the system. The scheduler uses non-preemptive priority scheduling, with priorities decided as follows:
- M1 uses priority of execution for the processes as P1 > P2 > P3 > P4, i.e., P1 and P4 have highest and lowest priorities, respectively.
- M2 uses priority of execution for the processes as P4 > P3 > P2 > P1 (without violating highest priority rule), i.e., P1 and P4 have highest and lowest priorities, respectively.
A process is scheduled to a processor if the processor is free and no other process with a higher priority is waiting with that processor. Assume each process has its own I/O to the processor to its own CPU burst.
What is the average waiting time of the processes in milliseconds?
A. 9.00
B. 8.75
C. 6.50
D. 7.50
questions.to_find: Average waiting time of all four processes under the described non-preemptive priority scheduling on two processors

understand.plain: We have two processors and four processes arriving simultaneously. Each processor has its own priority ordering. The key constraint is that a process can be assigned to a processor only if no higher-priority process (for that processor) is still waiting. We simulate the allocation and compute how long each process waits before it starts executing.

keywords.explain: Once a process starts on a processor it runs to completion; the scheduler picks the highest-priority ready process when a processor becomes free.
keywords.example: P1 (highest priority) always gets the CPU before P2, P3, P4.

keywords.explain: Time from a process's arrival until it first starts executing on a processor.
keywords.example: If P3 starts at t=16 and arrived at t=0, its waiting time is 16 ms.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 540 180' width='540' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='20' class='lbl ink'>M1:</text><rect x='50' y='5' width='100' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='25' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P1 (20ms)</text><rect x='150' y='5' width='50' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='175' y='25' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P4 (10ms)</text><text x='10' y='65' class='lbl ink'>M2:</text><rect x='50' y='50' width='80' height='30' class='ok' style='fill:#1D9E75'/><text x='90' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:white'>P2 (16ms)</text><rect x='130' y='50' width='125' height='30' class='no' style='fill:#D85A30'/><text x='192' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:white'>P3 (25ms)</text><line x1='50' y1='100' x2='50' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='130' y1='100' x2='130' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='150' y1='100' x2='150' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='200' y1='100' x2='200' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='255' y1='100' x2='255' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><text x='47' y='115' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='124' y='115' class='sub mut'>16</text><text x='144' y='115' class='sub mut'>20</text><text x='194' y='115' class='sub mut'>30</text><text x='249' y='115' class='sub mut'>41</text><text x='10' y='155' class='sub ink'>Waiting: P1=0, P2=0, P3=16, P4=20 => Avg = (0+0+16+20)/4 = 9 ms</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing M1 running P1 (0-20) then P4 (20-30), and M2 running P2 (0-16) then P3 (16-41). Waiting times: P1=0, P2=0, P3=16, P4=20, average=9 ms.

given.aim: Find the average waiting time for four processes under non-preemptive priority scheduling on two processors.

terms.meaning: P1=20ms, P2=16ms, P3=25ms, P4=10ms. All arrive at t=0.
terms.example: P1 needs 20 ms of CPU time.
terms.connects: Burst times determine when processors become free and when waiting processes start.

terms.term: M1 priority order: P1 > P2 > P3 > P4
terms.meaning: On M1, P1 has highest priority; it is scheduled first.
terms.example: If P1, P2, P3, P4 are all waiting for M1, P1 runs first.
terms.connects: At t=0 both processors are free; P1 (highest priority for M1) goes to M1.

terms.meaning: M2 runs processes in order P4 > P3 > P2 > P1 but the constraint says a process can only run on M2 if no higher-priority process (globally, P1 > P2 > P3 > P4) is waiting without a processor.
terms.example: P4 has lowest global priority; it cannot run on M2 while P2 or P3 are still waiting.
terms.connects: This means M2 must pick the next globally highest-priority unassigned process at each scheduling point.

given.plan: At t=0, both processors free and all four processes waiting. Assign the two highest global-priority processes to M1 and M2 (P1->M1, P2->M2 since P1 > P2 > P3 > P4). When M2 finishes P2 at t=16, the next highest unassigned process is P3 (P4 is lower priority). P3->M2. When M1 finishes P1 at t=20, only P4 remains. P4->M1. Compute waiting times from start times.

steps.title: Initial assignment at t=0
steps.apply:
Both M1 and M2 are free. All four processes arrive at t=0. Priority order (global): P1 > P2 > P3 > P4.
M1 takes P1 (highest priority): runs 0 to 20.
M2 takes P2 (next highest priority): runs 0 to 16.
P3 and P4 wait in queue.
steps.note: Since both processors are free simultaneously and all processes arrive at the same time, the two highest-priority processes get assigned.

steps.title: M2 becomes free at t=16
steps.apply:
At t=16, M2 finishes P2. Remaining unassigned: P3 (burst=25) and P4 (burst=10).
Global priority: P3 > P4. So M2 picks P3.
P3 starts on M2 at t=16, finishes at t=41.
WT(P3) = 16 - 0 = 16 ms.
steps.note: P4 must keep waiting even though M2 is free, because P3 has higher global priority.

steps.title: M1 becomes free at t=20
steps.apply:
At t=20, M1 finishes P1. Only P4 remains unassigned.
P4 starts on M1 at t=20, finishes at t=30.
WT(P4) = 20 - 0 = 20 ms.
steps.note: P4 waited 20 ms because both P2 and P3 had higher priority.

steps.title: Compute average waiting time
steps.apply:
WT(P1) = 0 (started at t=0)
WT(P2) = 0 (started at t=0)
WT(P3) = 16 ms
WT(P4) = 20 ms
Avg WT = (0 + 0 + 16 + 20) / 4 = 36 / 4 = 9.00 ms
steps.note: Answer is 9.00 ms, matching option A.

solution.result: Average waiting time = 9.00 ms. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Non-preemptive priority scheduling on multiple processors: assign globally highest-priority waiting processes to free processors; waiting time = start time - arrival time.
